videos.transcript: What's up guys, we're here in Istanbul, Turkey and this video we're going to show you the best things to do in this beautiful city. It's really a bustling place. There's 15 million people that live here so there's a ton of stuff to do, a ton of history. And make sure you watch till the end of the video because we're going to give you some of our top tips so you can have the best trip possible. Because we've been scammed a few times so we don't want that to happen to you. So enjoy the video! We are Zac and Ine. Full-time digital nomads from the United States and Belgium. We met while studying abroad in 2018 and have been traveling the world together ever since. We're here to inspire your next adventure, whether it's hiking in the Canadian Rockies, a road trip through the US, or backpacking across Africa. On our channel you'll find unfiltered videos of hiking guides and the best things to do in destinations around the world. Istanbul is located in the northwest of Turkey and is the largest city in the country. It's not the capital city of Turkey, but it's known as the country's economic, cultural, and historical center. Formerly known as Constantinople, this city goes back thousands of years. There are many historical places to visit as well as lots of interesting art, architecture, culinary heritage, and a vibrant local atmosphere. Try to stay at least three to five days in this bustling city to be able to enjoy the many things to do. We'll now dive into our top seven. At number seven we have the vibrant Istiklal Street. Istiklal is the busiest street in the city, comparable to Oxford Street in London or Fifth Avenue in New York City. This long stretch has a wide range of shops, cafés, restaurants, bars, movie theaters, and much more. We found ourselves shopping and eating various times here. It's the perfect place to try the local cuisine such as Kumpir, döner, or a traditional Turkish breakfast. So this is one of my favorite foods in Turkey and you should definitely try it. It's a "kumpir". It's like a baked potato basically with cheese, butter, and like all kinds of toppings. It's pretty good. So one of the best things to do in Istanbul in Turkey in general is to take an authentic Turkish bath. So we're about to go into the one behind us and it was established in the 1400s and then remade in the 1800s. The Turkish bath or Hamam is an important part of Turkish culture. It's a type of steam bath and also a place of public bathing. It was used primarily within the Ottoman Empire, starting in the 16th century. Its purpose is to purify your body and mind. This is achieved by first sweating and rinsing your body in a steam room for about 30 minutes. Then, getting a full body scrub to remove the dead skin. We weren't allowed to film the scrubbing part but trust us it can be rough. However, it's an essential part of the experience. The fifth best thing to do in Istanbul is to walk the Galata Bridge. The bridge spans the Golden Horn and stretches between modern Istanbul on the north and the Old Town of Istanbul on the south. The golden horn is actually an inlet of the Bosphorus river, which forms the border between the European and Asian continent. It was once an important trading post and still has a lot going on today. From the fishermen lined up on the bridge to the bustling Eminönü Square, and the new mosque on the south end. Topkapi Palace is an architectural highlight in Istanbul and therefore ranks at number four. Home to the Ottoman sultans for nearly 400 years, this palace was the heart of the vast Ottoman Empire. The palace has hundreds of rooms decorated with beautiful tiles, gold, and other decorations. Some rooms are more like a museum where you can find artillery from the Ottoman Empire. It's easy to spend a few hours here. So here at Topkapi Palace, we just explored the whole area and it's better to come later in the day. Because like a lot of places in Istanbul, this place is really busy, really packed, a lot of people. But if you come kind of around four or five you can enjoy for a couple hours and then catch a sunset. And it's really nice. The third best thing to do in Istanbul is to visit the Galata Tower. This historic tower sits high above the city and is visible from all angles. It was once used as a watchtower, but is now converted into a museum. Most people come here to enjoy the best views in Istanbul, but we also really enjoyed learning about its importance and use over time. It's possible to get an audio guide at the entrance, which we also highly recommend. The runner-up activity is to shop at the Grand Bazaar and the Spice Bazaar. Both are located in the old town of Istanbul, near the Galata Bridge. The Spice Bazaar is a giant covered market with a majority of shops selling food. You can find traditional Turkish delights and other delicacies here, such as olives, figs, and pistachios. The market is also close to the Grand Bazaar, which is the true center of hustle and bustle. With over 4,000 shops, the Grand Bazaar has a little bit of everything. From ceramics to gold, leather, and Turkish carpets. Indulge yourself in the local culture but don't forget to bargain if you want to buy something. The best thing to do in Istanbul is to visit the Hagia Sophia, located in the heart of Sultanamet. This is one of the most important and impressive buildings ever constructed. It was once the greatest church in Christendom and during the Ottoman Empire was converted into a mosque. Later on, it was established as a museum. But in 2020, it reverted back to a mosque. It's now free to enter, but to fully understand the importance of this building, we encourage you to go on a free walking tour. Free walking tours run daily at 10:30 AM. And in addition to the Hagia Sophia, the tour also passes by the Hippodrome, Blue Mosque, and Grand Bazaar. It's a great way to learn about the city and its history. Find more info about the tour in the description below. Hello guys my name is Yunus Emre. I'm official tour guide, we started four years. We provide great service for a sustainable tourism, uh I think you're enjoying our tour as well. We expect more people to come and enjoy on our tour as well. Thank you. Since Istanbul is so large, there are many more things to do in the city that we didn't mention in this video. some other highlights include a boat tour from the European to the Asian side, going to a rooftop bar or restaurant to enjoy the view, strolling around the colorful Balat, visiting the mosaic museum or archaeological museum, and visiting the Basilica Cistern, which was temporarily closed at the time of this video. As mentioned at the start of the video, we promised we'd share some things that we learned so you can make the most of your trip and have a great experience. The first thing we learned is that traffic is very slow. So try to avoid taking a bus or a taxi. The best way to get around is by walking or taking the metro or tram. However, getting a transportation card can be a bit challenging as the machines often malfunction. Get help from an official transportation officer at the ticket booth to get one that suits your needs best. Second thing we learned is that you really need to watch out for scams. Especially in the old town and around touristic highlights with many people around them. At first sight the people you meet in that area might seem friendly, but they often want to get you into their shop. Show respect but don't follow them if you're not interested in buying anything. We were pulled into a small shop ourselves in that area and had to buy pillowcases just to get out of the store after a lengthy discussion with the shop owners. Finally, Istanbul is a huge city with a large number of tourists. Keep in mind that most of the popular attractions have long waiting lines. For example, we stood in line at Topkapi Palace for 45 minutes even though it was the end of the day. And we had the same experience at Galata Tower. So it's best to choose a few activities for the day, especially if you have a limited number of days in the city. All right guys that's it for our video on the best things to do in Istanbul. Which activities are you most excited to do yourself? Let us know in the comments below. Make sure to like this video if you found it helpful, subscribe to our channel for more Turkey videos, and download our free giveaway in the description below. Catch you on the next adventure!

best things to do in Istanbul
videos.transcript: If you only have a day or two to see Istanbul, chances are you’ll hit the classics — the top 10 "I'll call them" "postcard activities": Hagia Sophia. The Blue Mosque. A stroll down Istiklal Cadesi Street all the way down to Taksim Square Climb Galata Tower, maybe. Though once is enough at $35 a pop. Get lost in the Grand Bazaar Grab yourself one too many samples in the Egyptian Spice Bazaar. Visit the stunning Topkapi Palace Go underground to explore the 336 columns of Basilica Cistern. And of course, indulge in the food. [Music] You’ll knock most of these out in Sultanahmet, the core tourist area of the Fatih district. As well as in Beyoğlu, the more modern center of Istanbul on the European side. And sure, they’re iconic. They’re beautiful. But they’re also crowded, catered mostly to tourists, and in many ways, exactly what you'd expect. But...if you can stick around a little longer —and I recommend at least a week - the city really starts to crack open. You start seeing Istanbul, not just visiting it. And even if you're here for a short time there are so many lesser known activities and more authentic experiences that you can do that locals also love doing. So here's my list of the top 20 things you should do to get a more well-rounded experience of the real Istanbul that most tourists overlook. And if you're looking for a comprehensive map highlighting our favorite food, coffee, and activities you can't miss, we'll have that linked below. Take the ferry just to take a ferry Besides the food, Istanbul's ferry system is our favorite thing about this city. For about 1 US Dollar, you can take a ferry to get to many of the different Istanbul neighborhoods and districts. The views are outrageous, we would sometimes just get on the ferry for the experience itself. And many locals do the same. There are three major ferry operators in the city: the municipally owned Şehir Hatları which translates to "City Lines", which operates traditional vapurs; the privately operated İstanbul Deniz Otobüsleri which translates to "İstanbul Sea Busses" which operates high-speed urban and intercity services, and the privately owned Turyol which operates mostly urban services.Taking a ferry between continents especially in the early mornings or late evenings during sunset is magical...and the city glows at golden hour. Visit Ferry Terminal Library Cafes Another interesting thing locals did that we loved was hang out at one of the many different ferry terminal libraries and cafes. Yes, can you believe it, their ferry terminals have their own subsidized library and cafe with reasonable prices and incredible views right on the waterfront. Something we never knew about but we're stoked when we discovered it. Everyone knows how hilly Istanbul can be, especially the Beyloğlu district on the European side. But what not everyone knows is that there's an epic shortcut that skips the big hill and takes you straight to Istiklal Caddesi street. All right, we are heading now to take the Tunel which is the second oldest underground railway in the world. It's uh basically a 90 second ride on this finicular that will take you from the bottom of Karakoy all the way to Istiklal Caddesi street. So, it's kind of like a shortcut to save you from that ginormous hill. Uh, which is honestly so clutch because on a day like today, uh, I would be drenched by the time I would get to Istiklal. And it's very affordable. So, it's a great way to save yourself that hill. Even exiting a steep. Yeah. This got us to Istiklal Caddesi Street. The tower is only like 400 m away. [Music] Explore the best coffee shops and antique stores in Cihangir. Cihangir is probably the coolest sub-neighborhood in the Beyoğlu district. It's known for it's cafes, antique stores, bohemian laid-back culture, an abundance of street cats... The only downside of this area is it's hilly AF. The streets are steep. To get to the heart of it, I recommend typing in "Berol Antik" in Google Maps to get you there. Maybe pop in to Cuma to try Cilbir, a popular breakfast dish that's delicious. But honestly, there are so many good spots to eat and drink, you can't go wrong. And like I said, when it comes to great coffee shops, Cihangir has them everywhere, here's a list of our personal favorites. [Music] Visit the Kusgun neighborhood in Uscadar. Kusgunduk, nestled in the Uscadar district on the Asian side, is a picturesque cobblestone neighborhood blending history, culture, and tranquility. It has a very mellow, villagel-like, neighborhoody vibe. We stopped several times to grab some truly outstanding coffee and had what we thought was the best manta in the whole city made by a very sweet older woman. Walk along their main street, Iadia Street, and you'll find many artisan bakeries, cozy cafes, antique shops, and unique stores capturing that oldworld vibe. I also recommend taking a stroll along the Uscodar Coast Walkway, especially during Golden Hour, where you can enjoy views of the Galata Tower across the water and get an uplose look of Maiden's Tower. Walk along the shore of Beck. Bec is an elegant upscale neighborhood on the European shore of the Bosphorus in Istanbul's Bashikbash district. Known as Bazun Guz B, the apple of the Bosphorus's eye, it has long been one of the city's most prestigious residential areas dating back to Ottoman times. This area has great coffee shops and restaurants, but what I like most about it is its coastal walk right along the waterfront. This is hands down one of the best running routes in the city. You can literally run all the way from Ortico Mosque to Beck Park. And if you want to take a dip in the Bosphorus, this area in front of Beck Park is literally the best place near the city center that's safe and protected from boats. From there, you can also walk south to a very charming area called Arnavitkay, which translates to Albanian village, or even head north to explore one of the many fortresses. They're absolutely stunning and another reminder of Istanbul's long history. Do a street food tour on the Asian side. A street food safari with a guide is a must here in Estambul, but I particularly recommend doing one on the Asian side in Kazik. Having a local show you around can definitely give you valuable insider information that you wouldn't otherwise know and also give you a good foundation to base yourself off of to explore on your own. Some top foods I 100% recommend you try are media. They're classic stuffed muscles that come either regular or spicy and somehow taste even better when they feed it to you. Tantuni durum stir-fried lamb, beef, or chicken served in these long skinny snorkel-shaped wraps. Lak majun, one of our favorites. A delicious flatbread topped with minced meat, vegetables, and herbs. Ped looks like a boat-shaped pizza made with wheat-based dough and topped with cheese, tomatoes, and the meat of your choosing. Manta. This is a type of dumpling found a lot in Turkish cuisine. Typically coated with a yogurt garlic sauce. So so good. Tersu. Turks know how to mix an acid into their meals. These pickled vegetables are often offered alongside dishes. The famous and very strong Turkish coffee served with finely ground coffee beans still in it that settle to the bottom. Do not consume that part. And of course try baklava and so many of their other desserts. And if you're more independent and want to go at it solo, consider doing a self-guided route, which I will be also making a video on in the weeks to come. On a side note, for those looking for great shopping in Istanbul, Kadik, in my opinion, has the best shopping and the best prices in the city. And fun fact, Zara and H&M actually make a lot of their clothes here in Turkey. Have a raka and mez night at a Mayhane. Istanul is synonymous with mayhan. A traditional local drink house that's the go-to spot to drink alcohol with mez. This is a must do local experience. Long dinners with various small plates, sampler style Turkish music, and the national spirit, raka. It doesn't get any better than that. You can either go to a more traditional maze bar or a more modern new wave mayane. Most tourists commonly go to rooftop bars in Sultanameit. Locals go to mayhanes. And don't forget to dilute your raka to your preferred level. Have a Turkish breakfast experience aka the sultan special with locals. If you want to know what it feels like to eat like a sultan and experience all the gluttony and hedonism that comes with it. Have an abundant Turkish breakfast called Cavala with some locals and have them lead the way. I recommend going to either the charming Jihunger neighborhood that I just mentioned or go to the main breakfast streets in Bishikash. Finish it off with a nice cup of Turkish coffee or chai. Get lost in Balot and Fener's colorful streets. One morning we chose to take the ferry from Kadikay to Fenire. The journey took us about a half an hour and the views as always were incredible from the ferry as you get to go a bit deeper into the Golden Horn and go under Galata Bridge as well as under Ataturk Bridge. It was nice to be able to get that unique macro view of Finer and Balot from the water before actually getting to visit it on foot. Known as the Jewish and Greek quarters of Istanul, these neighborhoods give you a true window into Istanbul's layered past. From Byzantine to Jewish to Greek and Ottoman, here you'll find lively cobbled streets, rainbow colored Ottoman houses, probably what it's best known for, charming coffee shops, lots of vintage stores, antique stores, and small, tastefully curated boutiques. We sat down to enjoy the AC at a really good independent coffee shop called Coffee Department. This area of the city deserves at the very least its own afternoon to explore. We did notice as soon as we arrived that there were a lot of tour groups that come here. It can feel a bit Instagrammy. Stuff like this feels a bit tourist trappy to me, but the neighborhood itself and its buildings are very charming. And you can just explore it at your own pace and skip stuff like this, but it for sure has a good selection of restaurants and coffee shops to choose from. Here's where we stopped for lunch. Perfect. Thank you. Just sat down at a spot called Kufta Bud. They specialize in meatballs. It came with all these toppings. Got some spicy onions, eggplant, tomatoes, grilled green peppers. Olivia went with the chesta meatball wrap. Came with some of this flatbread. It looks amazing. And then on the side, I also ordered an ishi chesta. Oh my god, look at the meat in there. Real spicy brown beef, onions, perfectly crispy on the outside, oily, rich, delicious. Get a full service traditional Turkish barber experience. The most insane barber experience I've ever had was in Istanul. It was a roller coaster of surprises. The actual haircut and shave took all of about 15 minutes. But what followed after was the real treat. Facial steam, exfoliation, ultrasonic, microdermabbrasion, nose wax, flame ear hair removal. Yeah, that was a bit of a shocker. Face and hair masks, some kind of spray, some kind of after shave, some shampoo action, head massage, face massage, neck massage, more head rubbing, that claw head massage tool that makes your toes curl, and the guy even gave me a back massage to finish it all off. I walked out feeling like I'd been reborn. Only in IstAnul, only at a Turkish barber. For the full experience, ask for a comp bakum, which means complete care, which will include cut, shave, wash, and afterare. [Music] Player watch a traditional game in a local cave or cafe. A cavehane directly translates to coffee shop, but is essentially a place where older people go to play board games for hours and drink Turkish tea. The younger generation usually just plays games at regular cafes. But this is one thing that immediately stood out to me and I just loved observing. So many people would play games with each other. No phones, just actual real connection. The two most popular games we saw being played were Tavla, which is Bagaman, and the tilebased game called Okay, which is part of the Ry family. If you're bold enough, maybe ask a local to teach you one of the local games so you can join in. I chickenened out, but I wish I hadn't. Here are a few bonus recommendations. I'll rapid fire through lightning round style. Have a traditional tea garden experience. Chai is everything to a stumble and such a big part of everyday life. They seriously drink so many every day. That's why I recommend you visit a traditional tea garden like Moda Alle Chai Bachesi for a very local way to enjoy chai. Sitting in white plastic chairs while sipping tea and looking out at the sea of Marmura. You've got accordion players and some meat sellers nearby. It's a vibe. Got my sea meat. Book a weekday morning slot in a hamom for the most tranquil experience. When visiting Istanbul, I recommend you go to at least one homam. This one was great, but probably not the most authentic, but it was the only one that allowed us to film. Don't forget to also check out the Arsta Bazaar right behind the Blue Moss. We have made it to Arsta Bazaar. It has a lot less people than the Grand Bazaar or Spice Bazaar, and the offering is overall better in my opinion. I recommend going to Jennifer's Collection for the best selection of towels, blankets, and robes. And when you go to the Grahams, instead of sticking to the main corridors, ask shopkeepers or follow side alleys to discover some of the hidden courtyards. Type Zenzir Lehan in Google Maps to get directions to one of our favorites. Some have rooftop access and secret tea spots. Don't forget to visit the Istanul Museum of Modern Art, the Dolabach Palace, and the Ort Mosque. Walk up to Chamluka Hill for a great viewpoint of the city. If you have more time, check out one of the prince's islands. Burgazada is the biggest one and swimming friendly but can get busy. Kinalyada has narrow pebbled beaches lined with white wooden houses. And Helada is the second biggest island and the one I was recommended the most by locals. If you do want to visit the underground Basilica Sistern, consider visiting it at night doing what's called the night shift experience for a surreal ambience with fewer crowds. The place takes on a haunting magical feel after dark with lighting and music. I also recommend doing a quick vintage tram ride down Istigla Jadesi Street, but I especially recommend doing it also at night. Rolling through the glowing chaos of Istigla Street on the old nostalgic red tram is an iconic experience. And lastly, one of my personal favorite free things to do is to just hang out with the fisherman on Galata Bridge while soaking in the stunning views. We did this when we were there in winter and really enjoyed it. I hope this video was helpful and gave you some unique ideas of things to do in Istanbul. I will be linking our full map below with every single food spot and coffee shop we recommend. Istanbul is truly an incredible city that I hope everyone gets a chance to visit at some point in their lives. And if you want to support our channel, please subscribe and like this video to boost that algorithm. [Music]

these activities so it's going to help you during your stay and also if you want to take your Turkish to the next Next Level we are always a few clicks away so speaking about expanding your vocabulary we have to talk about the this funny little sound is not a word in itself but it means so much in stumble and all over the country it's often accompanied by a set of raised eyebrows so the is the shortest and the most direct way of saying a few things it can mean no no there isn't sorry but we don't have those are you kidding me or simply I don't know and if you you ever feel like people are muscles steamed burgers and and cooco essential items on Istanbul's Rich Street Food menu and if you have some kind of a conservative alongside you who is negging about how unhealthy they can be just is the next step you must follow don't buy anything without a friendly hego prices move up and down at speeds you cannot even imagine in stumble so while shopping it is in your best interest to at least try to cut the prices a little just don't overdo it and you'll be fine who knows maybe with some luck that incredible carpet can be yours with half the price the don't get me wrong people will
